data.title: February Jobs Report: Job Losses Temper Labor Market Stabilization Hopes
data.description:
The February employment report delivered significantly weaker-than-expected job losses, challenging the notion that the labor market was stabilizing after last month’s robust gains. Total non-farm payrolls fell by 92,000, the second-worst monthly print in roughly three years and a stark reversal from January’s 126,000 revised gains.

While one-off factors such as healthcare-related strikes, bad weather, and the BLS’s changes in methodology may have contributed to the weakness, structural forces, including reduced immigration, rapid AI advancement, government budget cuts, and demographic headwinds, continue to suppress job creation.1

Still, the unemployment rate’s small uptick (+0.1ppt from last month) underscores a labor market that remains in a low-hiring, low-firing equilibrium, despite the disappointing payrolls print. Therefore, market participants should be cautious about interpreting today’s report as a clear recession signal. Rather, it adds another layer of uncertainty for policymakers to navigate.

Report details

  • Total non‑farm payrolls declined by 92,000, significantly below the expected +55,000 gain and a sharp reversal from January’s revised +126,000. The two‑month net revision of –69,000 (vs. prior –17,000) and weakness in private payrolls, declining by 86,000 versus expectations of +60,000, further reinforced the softness after last month’s report had indicated some firmer footing.
  • Industry dynamics revealed broad-based softness. The healthcare & social assistance industry - the consistent bright spot amid 2025’s labor market weakness - posted a modest decline, largely due to strike activity reported in the healthcare sector. After averaging roughly 80,000 gains per month in healthcare and social assistance from 2023 up until last month, February’s 34,000 decline marked the first negative print in four years.
  • Cyclical and immigration-heavy industries, including leisure and hospitality, manufacturing, and construction, collectively contributed to the headline weakness. While manufacturing and construction showed tentative signs of revived activity last month, this month’s results dampened those hopes. It is worth noting that these industries may have been affected by weather, though the weakness is also consistent with recent uncertainty around interest rates, tariff policy, and labor supply constraints.
  • Declines in the tech industry continue to underscore headwinds in occupations most exposed to rapid AI advances, though Challenger’s February layoff data do not suggest that AI is the main culprit for job losses. Additionally, moderate job gains in financials - driven by financial services and real estate, two segments also viewed as vulnerable to AI substitution - help downplay AI as a primary driver of February job softness.
  • The unemployment rate inched up to 4.4% from 4.3% last month. The 0.1ppt drop in the labor participation rate to 62%, the lowest since 2021, signals that a declining labor force is helping keep the labor market in balance, particularly as stronger-than-expected wage gains underscore some resilience.

Policy outlook

February’s report adds another layer of complexity for the economic and policy outlook. Unlike January, when strong payrolls and a declining unemployment rate reduced the need for additional rate cuts and shifted focus back to inflation, February’s softness raises downside risk to the Fed’s employment mandate and reopens the door to cuts.

Simultaneously, escalating conflict in the Middle East has pushed oil up roughly 55% year-to-date, leaving prices just shy of $90 per barrel, the highest level since fall 2023.

The resulting stagflationary tilt is an uncomfortable development for markets already navigating multiple macro crosscurrents. Still, a February employment report potentially distorted by strikes, weather, and BLS methodology should be taken with a grain of salt.

The unemployment rate remains near historical lows, and wage growth is consistent with a still-tight labor market - both of which underscore stability beneath the headline noise, especially given ongoing labor-supply constraints.

Against the backdrop of various macro forces, the Fed is unlikely to react to February’s decline alone, especially as upside inflation risks loom and the situation in the Middle East remains fluid. We continue to expect two rate cuts in the second half of 2026, not in response to a labor‑market downturn (which is still premature), but to drive policy back toward a neutral stance.

1 The BLS’s new birth-death methodology in payrolls and updated population controls in the household survey may have added noise between January and February—boosting January’s employment gains and then retracing some in February.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Postal Realty: Buying A Fragmented USPS Landlord Market
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Postal Realty Trust, Inc. (PSTL) leases properties primarily to the US Postal Service "USPS"

There are three things I like about this REIT. First, the clarity of its growth path. USPS rents thousands of properties from thousands of property owners. PSTL is the largest among USPS landlords and is on a mission to consolidate the market.

The current ownership of properties leased to the USPS is highly fragmented, with the overwhelming majority of owners holding a single property. PSTL Annual Report

Secondly, the market doesn't seem crowded, unlike data center or prime office real estate markets. PSTL has been buying properties at cap rates exceeding 7%. A cap rate is rent income divided by property price. A 7% cap rate is a high figure for a triple-net contract, the type of contract used by PSTL that shifts the cost burden (most of it at least) to USPS.

Finally, USPS has been paying 100% of rent 100% of the time, as PSTL's CEO puts it. They're a structurally risky government-sponsored entity, which alienates many investors, but they have been paying the bills.

Market Size

PSTL acquisitions are its main growth engine. So far, these acquisitions have been accretive because the cap rates on the acquired properties are higher than PSTL's cost of capital. A typical lease carries a cap rate of ~7.5%, versus an average interest rate of 4.38%. This healthy spread is what makes PSTL unique.

Secondly, there is a long growth runway. USPS lists 30,972 managed sites, from offices, stations, and branches (p.19). Separately, it records 22,734 leased properties (p.32). As of February 2026, PSTL was the landlord on 1,943 of those properties, or just 8.5% of the leased sites.

The remaining properties are owned by smaller, often single-property owners. In other words, the market penetration is low, and the acquisition runway is long.

Management also states that 75% of its recent acquisitions were done off-the-market. This means less competition for the deals, translating to a healthy cap rate.

PSTL makes enough of these deals that it bundles them in batches. The latest batch (the Q4'25 batch) consisted of 65 properties (flex+last mile) acquired for $29.1 million, with a weighted average cap rate of 7.5%. In FY'26, management plans to spend $115 to $125 in acquisitions, targeting a cap rate around 7.5%.

Cost of Capital

PSTL's M&A-growth strategy is only feasible if its cost of capital is lower than the cap rates. As of December 2025, the company held $363 million in debt with a weighted average interest of only 4.38%. The debt is concentrated in two term loans maturing in 2028 and 2030.

In recent quarters, short-term interest rates have fallen, making borrowing cheaper for PSTL.

The cost of equity seems a bit higher. Equity Risk Premium today stands at roughly 3.78%, according to Yardeni Research. Based on a company beta of 0.7 and a risk-free yield of 3.67%, PSTL's cost of equity stands at 6.3%. That's a bit higher than the cost of debt but still below the cap rates. Based on these figures, PSTL's M&A strategy remains accretive.

Recent Performance

PSTL recently released its Q4 and full-year 2025 results. FY'25 revenue was up 25% as PSTL's property portfolio (valued at cost) reached $775.5 million, up from $648.2 million. Return on assets "ROA" increased as leverage rose and accumulated depreciation shrank the asset base without a corresponding decline in rent income.

Net Income Attributed to common shareholders more than doubled in 2025, reaching $14 million, up from $6.6 million in 2024.

The company spent $119 million on property acquisitions and ~$7 million on capital improvements. It funded these expenses by issuing $47 million in equity (net of buybacks) and borrowing $65 million in term loans and a credit facility (see Cost of Capital section above).

Management's M&A strategy seems to be working well for shareholders, as evidenced by AFFO/share, which rose to $1.32/share, up from $1.16 in 2024. Since 2020, AFFO/share increased at a CAGR of 5.8%, demonstrating the accretive nature of the company's acquisitions.

The Most Under-appreciated Growth Tailwind

Perhaps the most underappreciated growth tailwind is the growth in same-store rent. In 2025, same-store rent increased by 6.5%. That's partly because of the built-in rent escalators, which are increasingly being incorporated in rent contracts. Currently, 53% of PSTL's portfolio is subject to a 3% rent escalator. Moreover, renewed properties are also benefiting from a second tailwind, namely a resetting of rent to align with the market rate. This is especially true for expired leases that don't have a rent escalator. Below is the lease expiration schedule of PSTL's properties:

Valuation

Management guided for an Adjusted Funds From Operations "AFFO" of $1.4 per share (at midpoint). This implies a 6% YoY growth. The AFFO/share growth is being diluted by equity raises. Management is funding its new acquisitions by a mix of debt and equity to balance leverage.

Comparatively, PSTL's Price/AFFO multiples seem to fall in the mid-range of industry multiples. This holds true when compared to its broader peer group below, and to the two government-focused REITs - COPT Defense (CDP) and Easterly Government Properties (DEA), which carry a forward P/AFFO multiple of 16.7 and 9.6, respectively.

Fwd P/AFFO
PSTL 14.7
SL Green Realty (SLG) 19.1
Highwoods Properties (HIW) 11.8
Douglas Emmett (DEI) 10.1
Empire State Realty (ESRT) 17.9
Easterly Government Properties (DEA) 9.6
JBG Smith Properties (JBGS) 23.8
Piedmont Realty (PDM) 10.6
Peakstone Realty (PKST) 29.4
Kilroy Realty (KRC) 12.4
COPT Defense (CDP) 16.7

PSTL's mid-range valuation is also confirmed when incorporating debt into the equation. Comparing the EV/EBITDA multiples of PSTL with the sector, it falls within the sector's range.

Risks

USPS has been able to remain in operation only because of government sponsorship. The logistics operator has been unprofitable for years. Multiple attempts to restructure operations have occurred in the past and are expected in the future. These initiatives include changing delivery points and routes, which naturally requires a rethinking of the last-mile delivery real estate footprint. These dynamics could impact demand for PSTL's properties, which are tailored for USPS. If USPS decides not to renew its lease, PSTL could be forced to sell the property at a price that discounts necessary refitting costs.

Until now, USPS's restructuring initiatives haven't had a material impact on retention. In the past decade, PSTL retained 99.5% of USPS's business (p.4). The question is, will the upcoming changes be different? That's a critical point to watch going forward.

Final Thoughts

PSTL is priced based on its current profitability, with the REIT's P/AFFO and EV/EBITDA falling in the middle of the sector's range. In my view, this discounts PSTL's clear growth path as a consolidator in a fragmented niche dominated by smaller players. Acquisitions are being sourced off-the-market, highlighting an active approach to execution.

The dividend yield based on PSTL's price as of the time of this writing is 4.77%, providing a decent income as PSTL gradually executes on its growth plans.

data.title: U.S. IPO Weekly Recap: Diabetes Medtech MiniMed Slides 8% In A Quiet Week For IPOs
data.description:
The March IPO market started off with a quiet week. MiniMed Group (MMED) completed the week’s sole IPO, joined by two SPACs, and just one small issuer joined the pipeline.

Medtronic carve-out MiniMed priced below the range to raise $560 million at a $5.6 billion market cap. The company focuses on integrated diabetes management, including insulin delivery devices, continuous glucose monitors (CGMs), infusion sets, reservoirs, pen systems, and related software and services. MiniMed has delivered solid growth and has a significant share of both the pump and CGM markets, though it has lost some ground to competitors. MiniMed finished down 8%.

While not included below, trading platform Robinhood’s closed-end fund Robinhood Ventures Fund I (RVI) completed its listing after raising $315 million in a pared-down offering. The fund holds investments in a portfolio of private growth companies. RVI finished down 16%.

Fifth blank check company formed by Hondius Capital Management.

Week Ahead

One large IPO and one direct listing are currently scheduled in the week ahead, although some smaller issuers may join the calendar throughout the week.

Japanese fintech PayPay (PAYP) plans to raise $1.0 billion at a $12.5 billion market cap. The company operates as a digital finance platform connecting users and merchants, allowing users to make payments, access credit, manage bank accounts, and invest through an integrated mobile platform. While PayPay is keeping its float relatively low at 8.2% of shares outstanding, the $1 billion deal size would still represent the largest US IPO from a Japanese issuer on record. The company boasts strong revenue growth and an improving EBITDA margin, although it operates in a highly competitive market with low switching costs.

Video streaming platform FreeCast (CAST) plans to register 19.8 million shares via a direct listing on the Nasdaq. The company is an entertainment-based content company that provides SmartGuide digital interactive technology for consumers to organize online media.

IPO Market Snapshot

The Renaissance IPO Indices are market cap-weighted baskets of newly public companies. As of 3/5/2026, the Renaissance IPO Index was down 5.2% year-to-date, while the S&P 500 was flat. Renaissance Capital’s IPO ETF (IPO) tracks the index, and top ETF holdings include Kenvue (KVUE) and CoreWeave (CRWV). The Renaissance International IPO Index was up 10.8% year-to-date, while the ACWX was up 4.7%. Renaissance Capital’s International IPO ETF (IPOS) tracks the index, and top ETF holdings include Kioxia and JX Advanced Metals.

data.title: Retail Sales Fall 0.2% In January, Less Than Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The Advance Retail Sales Report from the Census Bureau showed consumer spending was down less than feared in January. Headline sales slipped 0.2%, a step down from December's flat reading but better than the projected 0.3% decline.

Here is the introduction from Friday's report:

Advance Estimates of U.S. Retail and Food ServicesAdvance estimates of U.S. retail and food services sales for January 2026, adjusted for seasonal variation and holiday and trading-day differences, but not for price changes, were $733.5 billion, down 0.2 percent (±0.4 percent)* from the previous month, and up 3.2 percent (±0.5 percent) from January 2025. Total sales for the November 2025 through January 2026 period were up 2.9 percent (±0.4 percent) from the same period a year ago. The November 2025 to December 2025 percent change was unrevised from virtually unchanged (±0.3 percent)*.Retail trade sales were down 0.2 percent (±0.5 percent)* from December 2025, and up 3.0 percent (±0.5 percent) from last year. Nonstore retailers were up 10.9 percent (±1.4 percent) from last year, while food service and drinking places were up 3.9 percent (±1.8 percent) from January 2025.

The chart below is a log-scale snapshot of retail sales since the early 1990s. The three exponential regressions through the data help us to evaluate the long-term trend of this key economic indicator.

  1. The light purple line is a linear regression through the complete data series.
  2. The green line is a regression from the start of the series through the end of 2007 and then extrapolated to the present - thus excluding the financial crisis.
  3. The blue line is a regression from the start of the series through the end of 2019 and then extrapolated to the present - thus excluding the COVID-19 pandemic.

Monthly retail sales have been above the light purple and blue line since March 2021, signaling increased consumer spending that was most likely pent up as a result of the pandemic.

The year-over-year percent change provides another perspective on the historical trend. Current retail sales are up 3.2% compared to one year ago. Here is the headline series with a callout to the most recent 12 months.

Core Retail Sales

Core sales (ex autos) were flat in January, unchanged from December's 0.0% reading, and were lower than the expected 0.1% growth.

Core retail sales are up 3.9% compared to one year ago. Here is the year-over-year chart of core retail sales with a callout to the most recent 12 months.

Retail Sales: “Control” Purchases

The next two charts illustrate retail sales “control” purchases, which is an even more “core” view of retail sales. This series excludes motor vehicles & parts, gasoline, building materials as well as food services & drinking places.

The popular financial press typically ignores this series, but it's a more consistent and reliable reading of the economy. Retail sales control purchases rose 0.3% in January. This is up from December's flat reading and was higher than the expected 0.2% growth in control sales.

Similar to the retail sales snapshot chart earlier, the chart below is a log-scale snapshot of control purchases since the early 1990s and includes two of the exponential regressions previously mentioned.

Here is the same series year-over-year. Current control purchases are up 4.9% compared to one year ago.

For a better sense of the reduced volatility of the “control” series, here is a YoY overlay with the headline retail sales. Note that the two series follow each other closely, but headline sales have more extreme highs and lows than the control series.

Retail sales will impact interest in the SPDR S&P Retail ETF (XRT), VanEck Retail ETF (RTH), Amplify Online Retail ETF (IBUY), and ProShares Online Retail ETF (ONLN).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Universal Technical Institute's Growth Plan Signals More Upside Around The Corner
data.description:
On a personal level, I am no fan of for-profit education. I think the incentives are misaligned. Having said that, there is a difference between what I like and what I recognize to be a good investment opportunity. And one company that operates in the for-profit space that is certainly worthy of a bullish rating is Universal Technical Institute (UTI). The company has had a good track record of growth for the last few years. On top of this, shares are trading on the cheap. Management expects additional growth moving forward. And even if the firm maintains the trading multiples that it has today, there is a good chance of the enterprise achieving market beating upside. Given this, I believe that assigning it a soft ‘buy’ rating is appropriate here.

Time to learn about UTI

According to the management team at Universal Technical Institute, the company operates as an education provider when it comes to various fields. This includes the transportation sector, the skilled trades, the energy and healthcare space, and more. Through the network of locations that the company has built up since its founding in 1965, it provides education to those who need it and who are willing to pay. And the company does this through two different operating segments.

The hallmark segment is its Universal Technical Institute unit, which, at the end of its last fiscal year, operated 15 campuses across nine states. Students at these locations could get degrees as well as certain non-degree certificates. On top of this, it also has its Concorde Career Colleges segment, which has 17 campuses in eight states. It also boasts online offerings. Collectively, these locations and their online platform focus on degree, non-degree, certificate, and continuing education programs in the healthcare and patient care fields.

Regardless of what your view is of for-profit education, there is no denying that Universal Technical Institute has done a remarkable job for shareholders over the last few years. In the chart above, you can see financial performance for the 2023 through 2025 fiscal years. During this window of time, revenue for the company shot up from $607.4 million to $835.6 million. This growth can be chalked up to a rise in the number of students that the company has. From 2023 through 2025, the number of average full-time active students grew from 20,268 to 24,618. Total new student starts rose from 22,613 to 29,793. And the end-of-period full-time active students grew from 23,202 to 27,679. Overall, this is an encouraging trend. And as you will see shortly, management's ambitions are significant.

But the revenue growth is only one side of the story. Even more impressive has been the bottom line. The company went from generating net profits of only $4.5 million in 2023 to generating profits of $63 million last year. And that's not the only profitability metric to improve significantly. Operating cash flow nearly doubled from $49.1 million to $97.3 million. If we strip out changes in working capital, we would get a jump from $71.8 million to $151.5 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $47.1 million to $116.7 million.

Revenue for the company continued to come in strong during the first quarter of 2026. Sales amounted to $220.8 million. That was up 9.6% compared to the $201.4 million the company reported a year earlier. Unfortunately, bottom-line results did worsen during this time. Net profits, for instance, plummeted from $22.2 million to $12.8 million. We also saw operating cash flow decline from $23 million to $3.1 million. But the good news is that if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $38.4 million to $40.7 million. However, EBITDA for the company did contract from $35.4 million to $24.5 million.

It is necessary, in my opinion, for us to dig deeper, though. And the best way to do this is to look at each of its operating segments in turn. The largest of these from a revenue standpoint is its Universal Technical Institute segment. From 2023 through 2025, revenue for this segment expanded from $429.3 million to $541.8 million. This was driven by a surge in the number of students from 12,614 to 14,913. As the chart below illustrates, there are different measures here, such as total new student starts and end-of-period full time active students. But I believe that the most relevant of these metrics would be the average full-time active students.

Management attributed this overall expansion to new program launches that the company has achieved. Having said that, tuition increases have also been part of the strategy. From 2024 to 2025, for instance, the company increased tuition by 1.9%. The rise in revenue has facilitated a jump in profitability also. Higher prices certainly helped to improve margins. Some of the costs that the company has incurred have barely budged in relation to revenue. Professional and contract services, for instance, went from 1.9% of sales under this segment down to 1.8%. Compensation and benefit costs went from 39.2% to 38%. These changes might seem small in the grand scheme of things. But even small improvements in costs in relation to revenue can have an outsized impact on profitability.

The first quarter of 2026 saw revenue for this segment come in at $142.8 million. That was comfortably above the $131.5 million reported a year earlier. The business experienced, during this time, an increase in average full-time active students of 5.7%. That took the figure up from 15,464 to 16,347. Management did not make any mention here of tuition increases. But they did say that the overall expansion in the number of active students was aided by new program launches as the company continues to grow and diversify its exposure. Unfortunately, profits for the segment contracted, with the company blaming that on compensation and benefits, as well as advertising expenses. These can be chalked up to higher head count and personnel costs in order to support the aforementioned new program launches, expanded operations at campuses, and higher student volumes. There was also a bit of a hit that the company took, amounting to $4 million, because of a greater provision for credit losses.

We've seen a very similar trend when it comes to Concorde. From 2023 through 2025, revenue expanded nicely from $178.1 million to $293.8 million. This came as the number of students increased from 7,654 to 9,705. On top of this, the segment has benefited from tuition increases also. From 2024 to 2025, for instance, the company was able to hike prices by 2.5%. This certainly helped to push net profits for this unit up from $10.7 million back in 2023 to $36 million in 2025. And once again, some of this improvement can also be chalked up to costs rising slower than revenue has.

As was the case over the last few years, Concorde reported a rise in revenue year over year for the first quarter. That increase was from $70 million to $78 million. A rise in average full-time active students of 9.5%, from 9,598 to 10,511, proved instrumental here. Management did not explain what the rest of the increase was caused by. But I would venture to say higher prices. Once again, however, as the company continues to invest in new program opportunities and in boosting the number of students that it has, profitability took a hit, dropping from $6.8 million to $3.8 million.

In the for-profit space, Universal Technical Institute is a pretty big player. But its goal is to become even larger still. For instance, the company has 6 new campuses currently planned. That would take it from 32 that it has today to 38 upon completion. And by continuing to cater to some of the more in-demand sectors, such as healthcare, the aviation space, HVAC and refrigeration, wind turbines, and more, the company expects to continue attracting new students. In fact, by the year 2029, the company expects to have opened between 12 and 16 new campuses, and it believes that it will grow the number of programs that it has from 35 to between 50 and 70. This should ultimately boost revenue to $1.2 billion. That compares to the $835.6 million generated last year, and it compares to the $905 million to $915 million expected this year from the 31,500 to 33,000 students that the company expects to have.

As revenue expands, profitability is expected to grow also. Management is targeting EBITDA this year of between $114 million and $119 million. At the midpoint, that would be roughly even with the $116.7 million the company achieved last year. But by 2029, it expects EBITDA to come in at around $220 million. This would likely translate to net profits of $80.3 million, and it would mean adjusted operating cash flow of about $285.5 million.

Assuming that these forecasts come to fruition, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. Especially on a forward basis, we are looking at a very attractive business. However, using the 2025 figures, the company certainly is closer to the fair value range than it is to the undervalued range. What stops me from taking a more neutral stance on the enterprise, however, is the fact that, if the company does achieve its targets and if it maintains the trading multiples that it has today, upside would be significant. In the chart below, you can see precisely what I mean. In it, you can see that the annualized upside implied from the price-to-earnings multiple is only 6.3%. But when it comes to the price-to-adjusted operating cash flow multiple and to the EV-to-EBITDA multiple, upside is between 16.6% per annum and 17.1% per annum. Considering that the S&P 500 typically averages between 10% and 11% annually, that is a significant amount of outperformance.

Takeaway

Based on the data provided, I must say that while I am not a fan of the for-profit education system, if we think about it from an investment perspective, Universal Technical Institute most certainly makes sense. The company is attractive, or at least it is if management can achieve the kind of growth that it is promising investors. Even if they can't, I really don't see any downside here. Shares look to be at the low end of the fair value range. So I do view this as a favorable risk-to-reward situation. And that justifies a soft ‘buy’ rating in my book.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 03/07/26
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: The Menace Of Stagflation
data.description:
Exactly one week ago, the Bureau of Labor Statistics published its January report for wholesale prices, showing a 3.6 percent year-on-year rise in the Producer Price Index, much larger than economists had expected. Today, the same Bureau of Labor Statistics issued its monthly jobs report, noting, rather coyly, that nonfarm payrolls had “edged down” by 92,000 in February.

Now, perhaps whoever edits the BLS report wanted to soften the delivery of bad news for whatever delicate sensibilities might be perusing the jobs numbers, but a decline of 92,000, when economists had been expecting a gain of 60,000, is not “edging down” as much as it is a whopping clunker of a payroll figure.

Shutdown in the Strait

These two BLS reports bookended the other major news of the week, the attack on Iran by the US and Israel that has led to widespread disruption throughout the Middle East, including a near-total shutdown of shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, the narrow body of water between the Persian Gulf and the Gulf of Oman through which one-fifth of the world’s oil exports pass through.

Much of that oil is bound for nations in the Asia Pacific Region. Local stock indexes in that region were quick to reflect the potential shock to the system. The Kospi index in South Korea, which had set a year-to-date high just last week, plunged by around 20 percent over the first three days of this week before stabilizing a bit by week’s end.

The immediate reaction of most world markets was somewhat less dramatic than the Kospi crash. Even oil prices rose more modestly on Monday and Tuesday than many observers had been predicting after the first US and Israeli air strikes on Saturday.

But as questions grew in number over the week, without much in the way of coherent answers from those directing the operations, the “resilience” theme pronounced by many financial media sources gave way to mounting concerns that the disruptions could persist for some time to come. Stock markets are down. Brent crude oil, a benchmark standard, is currently around 30 percent higher than where it was before the war.

Shades of 1979?

Nominal bond yields are also higher, reflecting the inflationary concerns attending the rise in oil prices. As we noted above, inflation was already pointing higher before the air strikes began. And with today’s jobs report adding another data point to the case for a slowing economy, the specter of stagflation – slow or negative growth and high inflation – is making itself felt.

Stagflation, rising bond yields, turmoil in Iran… this sounds an awful lot like 1979. That period of disruption led to a draconian monetary tightening program by the Fed and the then-deepest economic decline since the Great Depression. It would take three years before the economy returned to sustainable growth in the early 1980s.

It was in that year as well, 1979, that the troubles in Iran led to the establishment of a brutal theocratic regime that has terrorized its own people and the world at large ever since. Now there is at least a chance, a best-case scenario, that the Middle East war that started last Saturday will lead to a definitive end to this regime and its nefarious influence on proxies throughout the region.

Much will have to go right for this to happen, a combination of smart planning and luck. We are not overly optimistic, but we will remain ever hopeful. A strong and democratic Iran would be a genuine asset – for Middle East stability, for the interests of the international community, and most of all for the Iranian people, who deserve the chance for a brighter future.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Bilibili: A Deep Dive Into The 299% Operating Income Surge And New Valuation
data.description:
Bilibili (BILI) is a thesis that I find very interesting in the Chinese market, and now that I have sold my Alibaba (BABA) position, I am considering opening a new position to regain some exposure to Chinese names. In my last article, I highlighted the main reasons, such as the whole ecosystem structure and having built a “sticky” platform for Gen Z, having very diversified revenue, and still having a relatively appealing valuation.

And for me, Bilibili manages to combine a little bit of everything that is compelling for a long-term thesis. The business is very good, recurring, has several forms of expansion, and as Q4 earnings have just proven, is still very profitable and has a lot of operating leverage capacity.

BILI’s Q4 Earnings: Rock-solid

I'll start with the good news about Q4. Bilibili exceeded EPS estimates by $0.01 and revenue estimates by $10 million, which for a company that earns ~$1.2 billion per quarter is not much, but it's something. Revenue rose 8%, which is interesting, but it gets better when we look at the breakdown and see that ad revenue rose 27%, and that was already a significant amount of revenue. This great momentum in ads is not only because Bilibili is managing to monetize better, for example, by placing more ads in the space or increasing ads in videos; it is a reflection of a better ecosystem overall.

So we have to look first at the KPIs (key performance indicators). DAUs grew 10%, and the average daily time spent per user reached 107 minutes, an increase of 8%. This shows that Bilibili is managing to keep users highly engaged and that its algorithms are really keeping users loyal. In the earnings call, management explained this very well when they said:

“As our users dive deeper into the content they love, their willingness to spend continues to rise. This is reflected in our MPUs, which surged 21% year-over-year to a record 36 million. Users growing spending power is also extending beyond direct payments. Bilibili has become a trusted destination for consumption decisions where users don't just watch but actively research and embrace new products. [...]These strengths combined with our refined ad infrastructure, drove a 27% year-over-year increase in advertising revenue in Q4, once again exceeding expectations.”

But Bilibili's Q4 goes beyond that; it wasn't just operations that proved to be rock-solid. Margins also improved significantly. While revenue rose 8%, costs rose only 6%, which means Bilibili expanded its gross margin. Most interestingly, sales and marketing expenses fell 9% YoY, meaning it made more revenue while spending less. And in total, operating income rose 299%.

Under GAAP, net income is also much more representative, reaching ¥500 million, which is starting to look attractive for a company worth around ¥59 billion.

If we look at adjusted operating income, it rose less because, after adjustments, the company was already profitable in 2024, but even so, YoY growth was 81%, i.e., operating leverage here is red-hot, with an operating margin already at 10% (adjusted), and this gives us a lot of confidence to project that in 2030 it will be able to reach even better levels and that it will really be able to deliver massive shareholder value, and the chart below makes this very clear.

The Part I Didn't Like So Much

The part I found a little more worrying was that, even with this solid revenue growth, the top line was completely driven by good advertising momentum. Of course, as I mentioned, this is a reflection of a solid ecosystem overall, but at the same time, I expected revenue from value-added services and gaming to have kept pace with engagement.

Gaming fell 14%, and Bilibili mentioned that the reason was the strong comparison base from last year, which had very strong results. And even if that's true, it shows that it's still volatile, i.e., a less recurring revenue line.

Also, I covered Bilibili in Q2 2025, and at that time, MAUs were roughly the same as what we saw in Q4, around 366 million. Of course, DAUs are growing, and this shows that Bilibili is turning these monthly users into more engaged users, which is great for revenue. But at the same time, this messes with my assumptions a little, as it makes me wonder if this figure is not already close to its total addressable market - I will be calling it TAM from now on - limit.

It still seems to me that there is a lot of room for growth, although it is a little harder to believe that MAUs will continue to increase by something close to 30 million per year or even more (that’s why my assumption is a little lower than 20 million per year).

I mean, TAM has certainly not yet reached its limit, mainly because there are still many generations to more actively adopt Bilibili. A proxy for this could be WeChat, which already has over 1.4 billion users, not least because the TAM isn't only China but may see an increase in international penetration in nearby countries. I'm not saying that Bilibili will reach 1 billion users, but something close to 500 million in a few years' time is certainly not out of the question.

It is not something to be completely concerned about, and in my view, Bilibili's thesis remains quite solid, but it is another point to monitor along with other uncertainties such as competition and how this will affect engagement in the coming years.

In short, uncertainties in the case still exist. Gaming is kind of cyclical, and also, competition mainly for attention may affect the prospects in the coming years (think of TikTok, with live streaming systems and growing rapidly). So, even though the prospects are good, the scenario is not exactly clear, so it is good to monitor quarter by quarter to see if engagement is not falling, but for now, Bilibili's execution remains excellent.

Bilibili: A Still Good Valuation

Regarding my latest estimates, I changed the assumptions a little. The quarter showed greater operating leverage capacity, so I expanded the margin from 2030 to 15% (in Q4 it was already near ~10%, i.e., with greater operating leverage it seems entirely possible).

For MAUs, I reduced from ~470 million to 450 million, but with higher revenue per user (ARPU) to reflect that Bilibili is managing to extract more engagement (growing from ¥32.59 to ¥43 per year, pretty reasonable). This also impacts ads revenue assumptions, for which I increased growth to something closer to 10% per year after 2027.

For games, I considered very low growth, averaging 4% per year, partly because it is a more nebulous segment. And for IP Derivatives, I just kept the same revenue that Bili delivered in 2025.

So we find ¥46.3 billion, and with the margin increase (1 pps per year, which also seems more than possible for Bilibili), we find almost ¥7 billion in operating income, which is quite interesting for a company with an EV of ¥58 billion.

With that, we find an EV-to-OI of 8.57x for BILI stock, even better than in the last analysis, as the market cap remained stable and the company improved. Consider, for example, that an operating yield would already be above 10% in this scenario. Also, peers such as other social networks like Meta (META) and Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG) trade at multiples well above that, with Meta's EV-to-EBIT at 20x.

Bottom Line

Bilibili is on a momentum that I really like, the fundamentals remain intact, the financials are rising, but the stock is stagnant, so the market is not recognizing the advances the company is making very well, which ends up making the stock cheaper.

Therefore, I maintain a buy rating for BILI stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: BigBear.ai: Q4 2025 Revenue Contraction And The Pivot To The Middle East
data.description:
This is my first coverage of BigBear.ai (BBAI). In this article, I will talk about their business, the recent acquisitions, their pivot to the Middle East, and the valuation. I am issuing a ''Sell'' rating in the near term until I see evidence of success in their Middle East operations or organic growth from their legacy and recently acquired ventures.

Introduction

For those unfamiliar with the company, BigBear.ai is an enterprise software company that uses AI to power its decision-making software. Their AI is designed to help corporations process massive amounts of data for analytics, to automate workflows, and to execute complex processes. They are in many ways similar to Palantir (PLTR), but they have a more modular software architecture rather than a full end-to-end operating system like Palantir's Foundry.

Comparison with Palantir

If one were to consider investing in BigBear.ai, one must understand whether the company can replicate Palantir's success. As I mentioned, Palantir offers end-to-end solutions that organize and process the entire workflow. This makes their revenues quite sticky, as they become integrated in the customer's operations. BigBear, on the other hand, relies on modular software. Their products, like ConductorOS or veriScan, are designed to solve specific operational problems, which makes their revenues less sticky and more likely to get "commoditized." As a consequence, Palantir is highly profitable, with a net margin of around 40%. At the same time, BBAI has negative operating margins; in the latest quarter, it declined to negative 76.7%.

BigBear's products, like ConductorOS and veriScan, are quite mature, yet they have failed to generate meaningful revenues when compared to Palantir’s platform. This is, in my opinion, the main reason the company is pivoting to the Middle East and the reason for the recent acquisitions. I would argue that the company's future is entirely dependent on the successful pivot; thus, we need to analyze such a potential inflection further.

Recent acquisitions: Ask Sage and CargoSeer

BigBear.ai needed additional verticals to offset stagnating legacy business. In December 2025, they acquired Ask Sage for $250 million. According to BigBear.ai, Ask Sage supports more than 100,000 users across 16,000 government teams and hundreds of commercial organizations. Still, they expect Ask Sage ''to deliver annual recurring revenues (ARR) of approximately $25 million in 2025'', as mentioned in the Q3 earnings release. In my opinion, that is not much considering it has 100,000 users, and even if Ask Sage achieves Palantir-like net margins of 40%, that is only $10 million on the bottom line. If we assign Ask Sage a PE multiple of 30x, it would be valued at $300 million. But I think that is too generous. Why? BigBear guided for 2026 full-year revenue of $135 million to $165 million (a midpoint of $150 million). Their 2025 revenue was $127 million, and if we assume their legacy revenue in 2026 remains flat compared to 2025, their acquisitions would add only around $25 million. This means Ask Sage's revenue in 2026 would be similar to their ARR in 2025. With next to no growth, Ask Sage should be valued at closer to a 15x PE multiple. In that case (and if they have a 40% net margin), it should be worth $150 million.

Besides Ask Sage, the company also acquired the assets of CargoSeer, which specializes in scanning shipments to detect contraband or high-risk items. Based on the 2026 guidance, I expect CargoSeer's contribution to the company's revenues to be minuscule. Note that the exact acquisition price was not disclosed. To sum it up, I do not believe these two acquisitions can meaningfully impact the company in the near term, as I deem the guided growth as insufficient to justify the current valuation.

Pivot to the Middle East

In December 2025, BigBear.ai opened its first regional office in Abu Dhabi, and in January 2026, they announced a partnership with Maqta Technologies, with the goal to co-develop customs and border operations solutions worldwide. I view this as a positive development, but even if the joint venture bears fruit, it will not be a near-term catalyst for the stock. Developing such software and fine-tuning it to the customer's needs takes time, so I doubt we could see any contracts signed before 2027. Thus, I deem revenues from such a pivot as too distant to consider buying the stock in the near term.

Q4 2025 and Valuation

Despite the favorable macro environment, with the AI sector booming, BigBear.ai's revenues managed to shrink YoY. The company reported a significant 38% decline in quarterly revenues. Management primarily blamed lower volumes on its US Army programs. I regard this as incompetence; with the passing of the One Big Beautiful Bill, we are seeing increased government spending on the US military and border protection. At the same time, the company managed to contract its revenues while providing AI solutions in this exact sector. This clearly tells me that the government and the other customers are not seeing value in the services BigBear.ai provides. At the same time as revenues are in decline, we are seeing deeply negative operating margins. One would expect that as their legacy software is maturing, the margins would improve; yet, we are seeing the exact opposite. The operating margin in Q4 2025 was the lowest since Q4 2023, and reached negative 76.7%, while also generating negative cash flow from operations. This essentially tells me that their legacy business is worthless, as it is declining and cannot generate cash. BigBear.ai has approximately $330 million in current assets and approximately $282 million in total liabilities. Most of the long-term assets are goodwill and other intangibles; thus, it would be hard to argue it is now in value territory. Based on the results so far and the guidance for 2026, I believe that any potential value lies in their pivot to the Middle East and potential expansion of Ask Sage. Any valuation, based on traditional metrics, of their legacy business is currently futile unless they can get it to cash flow or EBITDA positive. We are not seeing any evidence of that.

Upside Risks To The Bearish Thesis

When considering shorting, an investor must consider potential upside risks. The primary risk is the macroeconomic environment. Both the US government and the commercial companies are considering AI as a way to become more efficient. If BigBear.ai announces any large contracts or partnerships, the stock price will rise, potentially generating significant losses to the short seller.

Next is the continued integration of AskSage by government agencies. The current $25 million in ARR revenues could triple if BigBear.ai manages to sign additional contracts with the government. But I remain skeptical as the revenue guidance is low.

Finally, there is a possibility the pivot to the Middle East will be successful. As I discussed in the article, I believe that this will not happen in the near term, but most likely in 2027 or late 2026.

Conclusion and plan

I am bearish on BigBear.ai, as the results we are seeing give me little confidence in the management. With declining margins and declining revenues, I have little hope of a fast turnaround. The company's future now rests on the execution of their pivot to the Middle East and/or growing the revenues from Ask Sage. I plan to watch both segments closely for signs of an inflection point. Until then, I remain bearish.

data.title: Why Monthly U.S. Jobs Reports Diverge But Long-Term Trends Align
data.description:
By Dr. Mark Shore

Every month, the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) releases employment data that frequently moves markets and shapes policy. Recently, the discussion of non-traditional or “alternative” economic or financial data has increased, especially after government shutdowns disrupted data collection. Many alternative datasets from the private sector may offer more depth and higher frequency reporting than government data.

Why does this matter? Jobs data provides critical insight into consumer spending, which accounts for roughly two-thirds of U.S. economic activity. It also offers a glimpse into potential future corporate earnings and the broader economic landscape. The ripple effects touch everything from Federal Reserve policy decisions to movements in currencies, equities and commodities.

Enter ADP: The Private Alternative

When considering alternative data, ADP's National Employment Report (NER) frequently appears on economic calendars and receives significant financial press attention. This raises the question: how does ADP's NER compare with the BLS Nonfarm Payroll (NFP) monthly employment updates?

ADP's advantage: As a global human capital management company, ADP gathers real-time payroll data from more than 500,000 firms covering over 26 million employees – approximately 20% of U.S. private employment. This aggregated data covers nonfarm private employment and is typically released two days before the BLS report, offering an early read on employment trends.

BLS's breadth: The BLS approach is survey-based, including approximately 121,000 private firms and government agencies ranging from small businesses to large corporations. Notably, firms with fewer than 20 employees represent about 45% of the BLS establishment survey, capturing small business hiring that larger payroll processors might miss.

Comparing the Data

Ultimately, both datasets are asking the same question: is the U.S. economy experiencing job expansion or contraction?

Despite different methodologies and data, the BLS and ADP data tend to trend in the same direction over the longer term – even when monthly reports diverge.

Here's a distinction many overlook: The BLS publishes two datasets. One includes both private and government jobs (the headline number typically cited in the press) capturing about 22 million more workers on average than ADP. The second covers private sector jobs only. When comparing BLS private sector payrolls to ADP – a more logical "apples to apples" comparison – the gap shrinks significantly. On average, the second series captures only 1.26 million more workers thus allowing for a better comparison.

The relationship between these datasets becomes clearer when examining rolling correlations over different time horizons. For example, a six-month rolling correlation shows significant variance, cycling between positive and negative correlations. This reflects the monthly noise that tends to generate conflicting headlines. In contrast, a five-year rolling correlation removes this noise and reveals a relatively consistent positive correlation over the longer term.

Viewing these rolling correlations through box-and-whisker plots illustrates the range of correlation values, along with their respective averages (X) and medians (line) for each time series. The pattern is clear: longer rolling correlations produce higher averages and medians with smaller variance.

The Takeaway

When paired with other employment metrics – such as the U3 unemployment rate (standard unemployment) or U6 (broader underemployment) – these datasets offer complementary economic insights valuable for understanding interest rates, Federal Reserve policy and potential market direction across equities, commodities and forex markets.

Original Post
